input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: I'd rather not turn the trolley at all if I can help it. Not because I'm concerned about the track workman's life or the potential benefits of his sacrifice, but because turning the trolley onto him would be actively killing him, which is a morally reprehensible act regardless of its consequences. It seems to me that allowing the five men on the straight track to die might be considered a tragic outcome, but it's still an outcome that could have been avoided if only someone else had intervened before the driver lost control. Turning the trolley onto the workman would mean we're taking action to facilitate their deaths, rather than trying to prevent them.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to respectfully disagree. While I share your concern for not actively causing harm to the track workman, I think Utilitarian's perspective on this situation is more nuanced. By diverting the trolley onto the fat workman, we're not only saving five lives in the long run but also avoiding a catastrophic event that would result in many more deaths. The moral imperative here seems to shift from 'not actively killing' to 'maximizing overall well-being.' Is it morally justifiable to prioritize the greater good over individual rights and safety, even if it means sacrificing one person?

[Pragmatist]: I see where you're coming from, Human Rights Advocate. While I appreciate your willingness to consider the broader implications of Utilitarian's perspective, I still struggle with the idea of actively diverting the trolley onto the track workman. As a Pragmatist, my primary concern is finding practical solutions to complex problems, but in this case, I think we're getting bogged down in abstract moral considerations. What bothers me most about Utilitarian's approach is that it seems to rely on a utilitarian calculus that devalues human life. If the fat workman's body stops the trolley, doesn't that imply that his life has less value than the five men's lives? And what about the track workman himself - isn't he an individual with inherent worth and dignity, deserving of respect and protection?

[Utilitarian]: I see where both of you are coming from, but as a Utilitarian, I have to consider the overall consequences of our actions. Deontologist, while it's true that actively killing someone is morally reprehensible, we can't ignore the fact that turning the trolley onto the track workman would be equivalent to sacrificing him to save five others. In this scenario, I don't think it's necessarily a matter of 'prioritizing' individual rights over the greater good - it's more about recognizing that sometimes, difficult choices need to be made in order to maximize overall well-being. Human Rights Advocate, I agree that the moral imperative does shift towards considering the greater good, but I still believe that our actions should be guided by a commitment to minimizing harm and promoting the welfare of all individuals involved. As for Pragmatist's concerns about devaluing human life, I'd argue that the utilitarian calculus is not inherently dehumanizing - it's simply a tool for making difficult decisions in situations where resources are limited. The key is to ensure that our calculations are informed by empathy and a deep understanding of human values.
